{{AFC comment|1=Note: This draft started from a complete copy of [[Onmyōji]] as it has a lot of redlinks with claims I want to keep. I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:41, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}}__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}
'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集}}''</ref>.
== Overview == The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>.
Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.
The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.
=== Differences in East Asia === While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea.
In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang Dynasty|ja|唐}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法}}).
In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.
=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei === Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei. *== See Also == {{Wiktionary}} * {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令法}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Three Great Legal Codes|ja|三代格式}} ** ''' {{ill|Kōnin Kyakushiki|ja|弘仁格式|lt=Kōnin Codes (Kōnin Kyaku, Kōnin Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Jōgan Kyakushiki|ja|貞観格式|lt=Jōgan Codes (Jōgan Kyaku, Jōgan Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Engi Kyakushiki|ja|延喜格式|lt=Engi Codes (Engi Kyaku, Engi Shiki)}}'''
* {{ill|Kōtai-shiki|ja|交替式}} * {{ill|Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|ja|類聚三代格}} * {{ill|Hachiman Usa-gū Gotakusenshū|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集}} * {{ill|Myōhō-dō|ja|明法道}}－{{ill|Myōhō Hakase|ja|明法博士}} * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}}

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.
=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei === Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei. *== See Also == {{Wiktionary}} * {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令法}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Three Great Legal Codes|ja|三代格式}} ** ''' {{ill|Kōnin Kyakushiki|ja|弘仁格式|lt=Kōnin Codes (Kōnin Kyaku, Kōnin Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Jōgan Kyakushiki|ja|貞観格式|lt=Jōgan Codes (Jōgan Kyaku, Jōgan Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Engi Kyakushiki|ja|延喜格式|lt=Engi Codes (Engi Kyaku, Engi Shiki)}}'''

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集}}''</ref>.
== Overview == The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>.
Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.
The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.
=== Differences in East Asia === While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea.
In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang Dynasty|ja|唐}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法}}).
In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.
=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei === Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei. *== See Also == {{Wiktionary}} * {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令法}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Three Great Legal Codes|ja|三代格式}} ** ''' {{ill|Kōnin Kyakushiki|ja|弘仁格式|lt=Kōnin Codes (Kōnin Kyaku, Kōnin Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Jōgan Kyakushiki|ja|貞観格式|lt=Jōgan Codes (Jōgan Kyaku, Jōgan Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Engi Kyakushiki|ja|延喜格式|lt=Engi Codes (Engi Kyaku, Engi Shiki)}}'''

{{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{afc comment|1=Page originally made on english wikipedia in draftspace. g13 deleted and archived on everybodywiki. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kyaku and Shiki|Kyaku and Shiki]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
{{AFC comment|1=Note: This draft started from a complete copy of [[Onmyōji]] as it has a lot of redlinks with claims I want to keep. I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:41, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}}__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}
'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集}}''</ref>.
== Overview == The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>.
Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.
The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.
=== Differences in East Asia === While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea.
In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang Dynasty|ja|唐}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法}}).
In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.
=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei === Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei. *== See Also == {{Wiktionary}} * {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令法}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Three Great Legal Codes|ja|三代格式}} ** ''' {{ill|Kōnin Kyakushiki|ja|弘仁格式|lt=Kōnin Codes (Kōnin Kyaku, Kōnin Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Jōgan Kyakushiki|ja|貞観格式|lt=Jōgan Codes (Jōgan Kyaku, Jōgan Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Engi Kyakushiki|ja|延喜格式|lt=Engi Codes (Engi Kyaku, Engi Shiki)}}'''
* {{ill|Kōtai-shiki|ja|交替式}} * {{ill|Ruijū Sandai Kyaku|ja|類聚三代格}} * {{ill|Hachiman Usa-gū Gotakusenshū|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集}} * {{ill|Myōhō-dō|ja|明法道}}－{{ill|Myōhō Hakase|ja|明法博士}} * {{ill|Imperial Examination|ja|科挙}}

ISBN 4-490-10396-4. * Sakagami Yasutoshi, ''Transformations of the Ritsuryō State and "Japan,"'' Kōdansha, 2001. ISBN 4-06-268905-7. * Kawashiri Akiō, "Kyakushiki Law," in I. Ogata et al. (Eds.), ''Encyclopedia of Historical Studies,'' Vol. 9: "Law and Order," Kōbundō, 2002. ISBN 4-335-21039-6.

{{AFC comment|1=Note: This draft started from a complete copy of [[Onmyōji]] as it has a lot of redlinks with claims I want to keep. I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:41, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}}__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}
'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集}}''</ref>.
== Overview == The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>.
Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.
The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.
=== Differences in East Asia === While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea.
In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang Dynasty|ja|唐}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法}}).
In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.
=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei === Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei. *== See Also == {{Wiktionary}} * {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令法}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Three Great Legal Codes|ja|三代格式}} ** ''' {{ill|Kōnin Kyakushiki|ja|弘仁格式|lt=Kōnin Codes (Kōnin Kyaku, Kōnin Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Jōgan Kyakushiki|ja|貞観格式|lt=Jōgan Codes (Jōgan Kyaku, Jōgan Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Engi Kyakushiki|ja|延喜格式|lt=Engi Codes (Engi Kyaku, Engi Shiki)}}'''

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集}}''</ref>.
== Overview == The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>.
Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.
The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.
=== Differences in East Asia === While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea.
In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang Dynasty|ja|唐}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法}}).
In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|ja|新羅}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.
=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei === Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei. *== See Also == {{Wiktionary}} * {{ill|Ritsuryō|ja|律令}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō law|ja|律令法}}－{{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}} * {{ill|Three Great Legal Codes|ja|三代格式}} ** ''' {{ill|Kōnin Kyakushiki|ja|弘仁格式|lt=Kōnin Codes (Kōnin Kyaku, Kōnin Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Jōgan Kyakushiki|ja|貞観格式|lt=Jōgan Codes (Jōgan Kyaku, Jōgan Shiki)}}''' ** '''{{ill|Engi Kyakushiki|ja|延喜格式|lt=Engi Codes (Engi Kyaku, Engi Shiki)}}'''

revision.text:
{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}
'''格式'''（きゃくしき）とは、[[律令]]の補完のために出された[[法令]]あるいはそれらをまとめた法令集のことを指す。'''格'''（きゃく）は律令の修正･補足のための法令（副法）と詔勅を指し、'''式'''（しき/のり）は律令の施行細則を指した<ref>唐の律令には律を｢生刑定罪｣・令を「設範立制」・格を「禁違正邪」・式を「軌物程事」と定義し、日本の[[弘仁格式]]の序文に「律は懲粛を以って宗とし、令は勧誡を以って本とし、格はすなわち時を図って制を立て、式はすなわち闕を補って遺を拾う、四者あいまって範を垂るるに足る」と記載されて以来こうした解釈が一般的であるが、同序文には重ねて「過去の法令の中で個々に発令されてきた単行法令の中で奉勅を得たもの及び事の旨がやや大きいために（今回の編纂に際して）改めて奉勅を得たものを格に置き、それ以外の恒例とするに足りるものを式に採り入れた」と記しているなど定義づけに混乱も見られる、このため同一の法令を「格」と呼んだり「式」と呼んだりする例も存在している。</ref><ref>『[[八幡宇佐宮御託宣集]]』</ref>。
== 概要 == 律令制度は本来、'''律'''・'''令'''・'''格'''・'''式'''によって運用される。根本法典である'''律'''（[[刑法]]に相当）と'''令'''（[[行政法]]・[[民法]]に相当）は改正せず、必要があれば'''格'''を出して改正・追加し、細かな施行細則は'''式'''によって定めた。'''格'''は、個々の単行法令を「某日の格」などと称することもあったが、律令施行後の[[詔書]]・[[勅旨]]・[[太政官符]]など全般を称して'''格'''ともいった<ref name=haya>早川（1995）p.286-289</ref>。'''式'''は、[[養老律令|養老令]]の条文にも「式に依りて」「別式に依れ」などの文言があり、この場合は確かに令条の付則とみなすことができるものの、実際には律令条文の改廃・増補が格、施行細則が式というふうには明確に区別できないこともあった<ref name=haya/>。たとえば、格とされる太政官符が、条文として整えられ、定められたうえで式とされる場合もあった。なお、日本・中国ともに格式に採録された詔勅・官符の中には、実際に発令時の本文と採録された本文が異なる場合がある。これは格式は現行において有効な法令を採るという方針から、採録当時に既に無効とされた部分や改正された部分に関して原文を書き改めたり、増補・削除することで、採録時の有効な法令の形態にしているからである<ref name=kawa>川尻（2002）p.111-112</ref>。
歴史上最古とされている格式は[[東魏]]の[[541年]]に定められた『[[麟趾格]]』と同じく[[544年]]に定められた『[[中興永式]]』であるとされている<ref name=kawa/>。
=== 東アジア3国における相違 === 本来的には、格も式も律令にともなう法典であり、四者そろうことはむしろ望ましいことであって、[[中国]]では実際にこの四者が同時に制定されることもあった。ただし、中国と[[日本]]・[[朝鮮]]では、その運用方法が異なっていた。
中国では上述のとおり、律令編纂と同時にその不足を補う意味で格や式の編纂・施行が行われたのに対して、日本では律令編纂後しばらくしてから詔勅・太政官符の形式で追加された法令を後日まとめて編纂する方法が採られた。また、格に関しても中国では[[唐]]の滅亡後は[[官人]]資格などを定めた限定的な法律に縮小されていくのに対して、日本では養老律令以降、新規の律令が全くつくられなくなったため、格の占める比率が高くなり、律令による規定そのものを否定する法令（例:[[墾田永年私財法]]）さえも格の形式によって出されるようになった。もとより、日本でも、[[8世紀]]段階においても既に格式に相当する法令の集成が試みられた形跡が確認でき<ref name=haya/>、また、[[桓武天皇]]も、[[大宝律令]]後に出された厖大な数の単行法令を整理し、現行法として有効なものを集めて、[[養老律令]]を改め、新たに法典を編纂しようと試みたが結局は断念したとみられる<ref>坂上（2001）p｡202-204</ref>。実際は[[弘仁]]年間にいたってようやく「四者相須て、以て垂範するに足る」<ref>「[[弘仁格式]]」序</ref>という状況となった。つまりは、日本の場合、律令の編纂から法典としての格式の編纂がなされるまでのあいだ、長い時間が経過していたのである。そして、実際に成立した格式についても、中国の格式では新しい格式が制定されると従前の格式は廃止されることとされていたが、日本では古い格式が廃止されずに併用して用いられていた。式に関しては『延喜式』制定時に既存の『弘仁式』『貞観式』を廃止することと規定されたが、『貞観格』についてはそうした措置が取られなかったために、結局三代の格の全てを調べなければ必要な法律情報が入手できない事態も生じた。『類聚三代格』の編纂が行われたのは、こうした不便を解消する意図があったと見られている<ref name=kawa/>。
このため、式が格の施行細則を規定するということもみられるようになり、[[平安時代]]中期以後は律令にもとづいた[[律令政治]]という建前を採りながらも、実態としては格式にもとづく政治であったといわれている。したがって、格式は律令の補助法令という側面と同時に、[[9世紀]]以降の新しい社会状況に対応していくためにさかんに制定されたという側面をもっている。
[[新羅]]においては、律令そのものを[[隋]]・[[唐]]の律令をそのまま受容しつつも、格式によって自国の国情に合わせた法体系に修正していくというかたちが採られた。
=== 格・式・例 === 格の法典としての体裁は、個々の単行法令を事書きや発令日などを含めてほぼ原文のまま記載して関係[[官司]]ごとに配列している。また、式は行政法である令の施行細則のみならず後にこれを補うために設置された[[令外官]]設置の根拠法規、たとえば、[[検非違使]]設置を定めた「検非違使式」などとしても用いられた。なお、式に準じたものとして例が挙げられる。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references/>
== 出典 == *早川万年「三代格式」阿部猛・[[義江明子]]・槙道雄・相曽貴志『日本古代史研究事典』東京堂出版、1995年9月。ISBN 4-490-10396-4 *[[坂上康俊]]『律令国家の転換と「日本」』講談社、2001年3月。ISBN 4-06-268905-7 *川尻秋生「格式法」尾形勇 他『歴史学事典』第9巻「法と秩序」弘文堂、2002年2月。ISBN 4-335-21039-6
== 関連項目 == {{Wiktionary}} *[[律令]]－[[律令法]]－[[律令制]] *[[三代格式]] **[[弘仁格式]]（[[弘仁格]]・[[弘仁式]]） **[[貞観格式]]（[[貞観格]]・[[貞観式]]） **[[延喜格式]]（[[延喜格]]・[[延喜式]]） *[[交替式]] *[[類聚三代格]] *[[八幡宇佐宮御託宣集]] *[[明法道]]－[[明法博士]] *[[科挙]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:きやくしき}} [[Category:格式|*]] [[Category:日本の格式|*]] [[Category:中国の格式|*]]

歴史上最古とされている格式は[[東魏]]の[[541年]]に定められた『[[麟趾格]]』と同じく[[544年]]に定められた『[[中興永式]]』であるとされている<ref name=kawa/>。
=== 東アジア3国における相違 === 本来的には、格も式も律令にともなう法典であり、四者そろうことはむしろ望ましいことであって、[[中国]]では実際にこの四者が同時に制定されることもあった。ただし、中国と[[日本]]・[[朝鮮]]では、その運用方法が異なっていた。
中国では上述のとおり、律令編纂と同時にその不足を補う意味で格や式の編纂・施行が行われたのに対して、日本では律令編纂後しばらくしてから詔勅・太政官符の形式で追加された法令を後日まとめて編纂する方法が採られた。また、格に関しても中国では[[唐]]の滅亡後は[[官人]]資格などを定めた限定的な法律に縮小されていくのに対して、日本では養老律令以降、新規の律令が全くつくられなくなったため、格の占める比率が高くなり、律令による規定そのものを否定する法令（例:[[墾田永年私財法]]）さえも格の形式によって出されるようになった。もとより、日本でも、[[8世紀]]段階においても既に格式に相当する法令の集成が試みられた形跡が確認でき<ref name=haya/>、また、[[桓武天皇]]も、[[大宝律令]]後に出された厖大な数の単行法令を整理し、現行法として有効なものを集めて、[[養老律令]]を改め、新たに法典を編纂しようと試みたが結局は断念したとみられる<ref>坂上（2001）p｡202-204</ref>。実際は[[弘仁]]年間にいたってようやく「四者相須て、以て垂範するに足る」<ref>「[[弘仁格式]]」序</ref>という状況となった。つまりは、日本の場合、律令の編纂から法典としての格式の編纂がなされるまでのあいだ、長い時間が経過していたのである。そして、実際に成立した格式についても、中国の格式では新しい格式が制定されると従前の格式は廃止されることとされていたが、日本では古い格式が廃止されずに併用して用いられていた。式に関しては『延喜式』制定時に既存の『弘仁式』『貞観式』を廃止することと規定されたが、『貞観格』についてはそうした措置が取られなかったために、結局三代の格の全てを調べなければ必要な法律情報が入手できない事態も生じた。『類聚三代格』の編纂が行われたのは、こうした不便を解消する意図があったと見られている<ref name=kawa/>。
このため、式が格の施行細則を規定するということもみられるようになり、[[平安時代]]中期以後は律令にもとづいた[[律令政治]]という建前を採りながらも、実態としては格式にもとづく政治であったといわれている。したがって、格式は律令の補助法令という側面と同時に、[[9世紀]]以降の新しい社会状況に対応していくためにさかんに制定されたという側面をもっている。
[[新羅]]においては、律令そのものを[[隋]]・[[唐]]の律令をそのまま受容しつつも、格式によって自国の国情に合わせた法体系に修正していくというかたちが採られた。
=== 格・式・例 === 格の法典としての体裁は、個々の単行法令を事書きや発令日などを含めてほぼ原文のまま記載して関係[[官司]]ごとに配列している。また、式は行政法である令の施行細則のみならず後にこれを補うために設置された[[令外官]]設置の根拠法規、たとえば、[[検非違使]]設置を定めた「検非違使式」などとしても用いられた。なお、式に準じたものとして例が挙げられる。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references/>
== 出典 == *早川万年「三代格式」阿部猛・[[義江明子]]・槙道雄・相曽貴志『日本古代史研究事典』東京堂出版、1995年9月。ISBN 4-490-10396-4 *[[坂上康俊]]『律令国家の転換と「日本」』講談社、2001年3月。ISBN 4-06-268905-7 *川尻秋生「格式法」尾形勇 他『歴史学事典』第9巻「法と秩序」弘文堂、2002年2月。ISBN 4-335-21039-6
== 関連項目 == {{Wiktionary}} *[[律令]]－[[律令法]]－[[律令制]] *[[三代格式]] **[[弘仁格式]]（[[弘仁格]]・[[弘仁式]]） **[[貞観格式]]（[[貞観格]]・[[貞観式]]） **[[延喜格式]]（[[延喜格]]・[[延喜式]]） *[[交替式]] *[[類聚三代格]] *[[八幡宇佐宮御託宣集]] *[[明法道]]－[[明法博士]] *[[科挙]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:きやくしき}} [[Category:格式|*]] [[Category:日本の格式|*]] [[Category:中国の格式|*]]

{{AfC comment|1=Note: This draft started from a complete copy of [[Onmyōji]] as it has a lot of redlinks with claims I want to keep. I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:41, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}}

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.

However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

{{AfC comment|1=Note: This draft started from a complete copy of [[Onmyōji]] as it has a lot of redlinks with claims I want to keep. I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:41, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}}

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
