__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the [[:en:Traditional Chinese law|Ritsuryō]] system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]]. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang Dynasty]]'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under [[:en:Silla|Silla]], the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

I will trim down into just the claim on an individual thing later[[User:Immanuelle|'''Immanuel'''le]] ❤️💚💙 [[User_talk:Immanuelle|(talk to the cutest Wikipedian)]] 01:41, 25 April 2023 (UTC)}}

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

 {{afc comment|1=to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Kyaku and Shiki]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Kyaku and Shiki|Kyaku and Shiki]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}}



__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system. Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

__NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|WD=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|WD=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
*

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|WD=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} system. __NOINDEX__{{See Wiktionary|律令制における法令の一種}}

'''Kyaku-shiki''' (格式, '''{{ill|Kyaku and Shiki|lt=laws and regulations|ja|格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyaku and Shiki|qq=}}''') refers to supplementary legal codes or legal compilations issued to complement the {{ill|Traditional Chinese law|en|Traditional Chinese law|qid=Q4380197|lt=Ritsuryō|1=Traditional Chinese law}} system. '''Kyaku''' (格) were laws (auxiliary statutes) and imperial edicts meant to revise or supplement the Ritsuryō, while '''Shiki''' (式/のり) were detailed regulations for implementing the Ritsuryō<ref>In Tang China, the Ritsuryō codes were defined as follows: Ritsu (律) "regulates crimes and punishments," Ryō (令) "sets norms and rules," Kyaku (格) "prohibits wrongdoings," and Shiki (式) "establishes procedures." Japan’s {{ill|Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja|弘仁格式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōnin Kyaku-Shiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 三代格式|1=Sandai Kyakushiki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11354355}} preface explains, "Ritsu is based on discipline, Ryō on admonishment, Kyaku establishes laws as per the times, and Shiki supplements omissions." However, definitions were inconsistent, with some laws being referred to as Kyaku or Shiki depending on context.</ref><ref>''{{ill|Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|ja|八幡宇佐宮御託宣集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Usa Shrine Oracles Collection|qq=}}''</ref>.

== Overview ==
The Ritsuryō system operated on '''Ritsu''' (律, criminal law), '''Ryō''' (令, administrative and civil law), '''Kyaku''' (格), and '''Shiki''' (式). The core laws (Ritsu and Ryō) were rarely amended; instead, Kyaku laws were issued to revise or add provisions, and Shiki laws provided detailed implementation guidelines. Individual laws were sometimes called "Kyaku of [a specific date]," and decrees or government orders were also broadly referred to as Kyaku<ref name="haya">Hayakawa (1995), pp. 286-289</ref>. 

Shiki often supplemented Ryō with phrases like "follow the Shiki" appearing in the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|qid=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}}. However, in practice, distinctions between Kyaku and Shiki were not always clear, as some Kyaku laws were codified and regulated under Shiki<ref name="haya"/>. Additionally, discrepancies existed between the original texts of imperial edicts or official orders and their versions recorded in Kyaku-Shiki due to updates or revisions during compilation to reflect the effective laws at the time<ref name="kawa">Kawashiri (2002), pp. 111-112</ref>.

The earliest known Kyaku-Shiki codes were the {{ill|Linzhi Kyaku|ja|麟趾格|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Linzhi Kyaku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 541 and the {{ill|Zhongxing Yongshi|ja|中興永式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhongxing Yongshi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of 544, both in China<ref name="kawa"/>.

=== Differences in East Asia ===
While Kyaku and Shiki were essential supplements to the Ritsuryō system, their application varied across China, Japan, and Korea. 

In China, Kyaku and Shiki were often compiled and enacted alongside Ritsu and Ryō. However, in Japan, new laws issued via imperial decrees or government orders were later compiled into Kyaku-Shiki. Moreover, after the {{ill|Tang dynasty|en|Tang dynasty|qid=Q9683|lt=Tang Dynasty|1=Tang dynasty}}'s fall, China's Kyaku laws became more limited in scope, whereas in Japan, Kyaku laws played an increasingly significant role, sometimes even overriding the Ritsuryō system (e.g., the {{ill|Konden Einen Shizai Law|ja|墾田永年私財法|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konden Einen Shizai Law|qq=}}).

In Korea, particularly under {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|qid=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}}, the Ritsuryō codes were adopted from China, but Kyaku-Shiki laws were tailored to local circumstances.

=== Kyaku, Shiki, and Rei ===
Kyaku laws were typically arranged by department or office, with individual provisions including issue dates. Shiki not only provided implementation details for Ryō but also established rules for new offices, such as the {{ill|Office of Police and Justice|ja|検非違使|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Office of Police and Justice|qq=}} (e.g., "Kenpeishi Shiki"). Similar to Shiki, there were also supplementary codes referred to as Rei.
